inline_comments.message: I suppose you should put a proper ONAP's Jira ticket here.
Please click on the link and see where it's pointing, some dummy ticket. Just create proper one.
I did. I just had wrong ticket number here, corrected (i hope).

Is substituting /usr/local/bin/fakeshell.sh with 'sh' intended?

[root@installer ansible]# ./run_playbook.sh (...)
INFO: Bind mounting of external mounts...
(...)
INFO: Mount: /etc/hosts -> /etc/hosts
chroot: failed to run command ‘sh’: No such file or directory
Well, generally yes, it is intended. it should run sh -c something, so what happened?
when you rerun this script with this guard then you dont get inside this block to setup the CHROOT_ variables, right? Does this work? Or do the exports populate the env of unshare?
Well, yes, exports populate the environment and unshare command is not like sudo, it does not clear it, so the variables are passed through.
these temp directories are never cleaned up - and .overlay is leaving files behind - this looks like error to me - how the following overlay mounts deal with non-empty working dir?
why did you removed mode from this? I know we are running everything as root inside but that can change, I see no hurt in it to stay as before

this fails when workdir inside the chroot does not exist - previous version ensured creation of such path

Also this line seems overly cryptic - IMHO fakeshell.sh made it more readable
seems to work. Even if it didn't, you can probably turn workdir into tmpfs mount too, so we would not care about overlay leaving anything there.

my main reasoning was probably all this magic required to fetch the tty gid. There is however another reason: pty's are managed by the *kernel*. the user opening /dev/ptmx gets the pty master, and a slave is created in /dev/pts, and this slave is owned by the user that just opened ptmx. it is in particular not owned by root. And I do not see any reason as to why we would need to have any normal user in the tty group, especially if he can access his own terminals.

and more complicated. this is the other side, uploading your own shell script to do such things seems to me like an overkill, especially if the number of commands executed in that env has dramatically decreased. However i may make it more readable.
true, I did not notice that default mode is 600

This is so unnecessary. I had to use this technique for stdout only once in my life and here it is not even needed...and it looks very obscure.

how is this any different from just running mini script inside the chroot and without any copying to the two descriptors?

Your version:
unshare
   -> /bin/sh <heredoc> (and descriptor magic)

Previous version:
unshare
   -> /bin/sh fakeshell.sh (no magic)

I dont see benefits from this approach which would compensate the complexity and piping worked before as expected.

I will not -1 this, but I dont like it - it is just unnecessarily obscure and not everyone will understand why and what it does.

that is why I added a comment above, about redirecting stdin and why i have to do it. it is the same as those exec trick to redirect output to logs i saw in another commit. There is also a comment above the final exec line about having to restore it back. so at least the *why* seems covered to me.
Of course, i could have a small script copied to chroot, but I do not like copying something created by hand just to run it as opposed to running without copying.

I am quite sure that bunch of people never saw this and they will be banging their head why and what is that number three and that weird syntax around it.

It is not common and people know mostly only simple redirections like: > < >> << 2>&1 >&2 because that is the only usage they will come accross on regular basis. And also they dont even realize that some file descriptors are involved with these.

I am just saying that in this case it is overkill - actually no, it is not overkill becaue it does not bring anything new to the table - it does the same and provides the same but with an alien syntax. It just obscures semantics. You dont like previous version, which is fine, everyone has different tastes.
